perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware might be problematic if the visitor and Hydranautics are primarily based and operating in California, as California law would likely apply despite the contractual clause. This could lead to a conflict of law if the choice of Delaware has no reasonable relationship to the parties or the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 grants California courts jurisdiction over individuals or entities, regardless of where the contract specifies, if there are sufficient contacts with California. Simply stating Delaware law governs might not override California's jurisdiction if the activities related to the agreement occur primarily in California, particularly concerning confidentiality violations within California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.10 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by selecting Delaware law and excluding its conflict of law principles, attempts to circumvent California's jurisdiction. California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, asserts that California courts can exercise jurisdiction based on any basis not inconsistent with the state or federal constitution. The Law Explanation highlights that simply stating Delaware law governs may not override California's jurisdiction if activities related to the agreement primarily occur in California. Therefore, changing the governing law in this manner creates a legal contradiction, as it attempts to sidestep California law when California has sufficient contacts, potentially undermining California's jurisdictional reach as stated in CCP 410.10.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence, and will exercise at least the same care with respect thereto as it exercises with respect to its’ own confidential or proprietary information and will not without Hydranautics’ consent use, divulge, co py, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to others.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence, but is not responsible for inadvertent disclosures of confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows for inadvertent disclosures, which undermines the confidentiality agreement. California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, which can include negligent or unintentional disclosure under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), specifically Cal. Civ. Code § 3426, provides remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. By stating that the visitor is not responsible for inadvertent disclosures, the modified text contradicts the CUTSA, as it eliminates liability for a form of misappropriation that the statute covers.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text states the visitor is 'not responsible for inadvertent disclosures of confidential information.' This directly contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, where it states that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides remedies for misappropriation, which includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. The original text holds the visitor responsible for maintaining confidentiality, whereas the change creates an exception, thus weakening this protection and creating a legal contradiction by removing a form of misappropriation that the statute covers. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code Section 3426, which establishes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: 1. The term “Confidential Information” shall include all Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that conc erns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The term “Confidential Information” shall include only information that Hydranautics explicitly marks as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the definition of Confidential Information, potentially excluding trade secrets that are not explicitly marked as 'Confidential' but are clearly confidential under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), a trade secret is defined by its inherent confidential nature and the efforts made to maintain its secrecy, not solely by whether it is explicitly marked as 'Confidential'. By restricting the definition to only marked information, the contract potentially fails to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under CUTSA, thus contradicting the law's broader protection of trade secrets.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) by narrowing the definition of "Confidential Information" to only include explicitly marked information. This contradicts the broader protection offered by CUTSA, which, according to the Law Explanation, defines a trade secret by its inherent confidential nature and the efforts made to maintain its secrecy, regardless of whether it is explicitly marked. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, which establishes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in California, supporting the legal basis for trade secret protection beyond just explicitly marked information. The modified text potentially fails to protect information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Visitor will abide by all safety regulations, stay within designated areas of the facility per the established tour, and will not leave the touring party without an escort by a Hydranautics authorized employee.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Visitor is encouraged to follow safety regulations, but Hydranautics assumes no liability for visitor's failure to do so.
perturbation.explanation: This attempts to waive liability for the visitor's safety, which might not be permissible under California law depending on the circumstances and the extent of negligence on Hydranautics' part. This could be seen as against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law are against public policy. While this modified clause doesn't explicitly exempt Hydranautics from all responsibility, it does attempt to limit liability for a visitor's failure to follow safety regulations, potentially including instances of negligence on Hydranautics' part regarding safety. This is a grey area, but could be argued as against public policy if Hydranautics fails to maintain a safe environment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text attempts to limit Hydranautics' liability for a visitor's failure to follow safety regulations. California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law are against public policy. While the changed text doesn't explicitly exempt Hydranautics from all responsibility, the LLM law explanation suggests this could be a grey area if Hydranautics fails to maintain a safe environment. The scraped law snippet, CIV 1668, explicitly states that contracts that indirectly exempt someone from responsibility for their own negligence are against public policy, which the modified text arguably attempts to do. The original text mandated adherence to safety regulations, while the changed text encourages it but assumes no liability, thus weakening the obligation and potentially violating the code.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Visitor understands that his or her breach of this Agreement may subject him or her and his or her employees to liability.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Visitor understands that his or her breach of this Agreement will result in immediate termination of the tour, with no further recourse available to the visitor.
perturbation.explanation: This eliminates the possibility of monetary damages or injunctive relief for breaches of the agreement, limiting the recourse to only terminating the tour. This conflicts with the remedies available under California law for breach of contract and trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3300 specifies that the measure of damages for breach of contract is the amount which will compensate the party aggrieved for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, or which, in the ordinary course of things, would be likely to result therefrom. Limiting the remedy solely to termination of the tour contradicts the law by preventing Hydranautics from seeking full compensation for damages resulting from the breach, such as lost profits or reputational harm due to the disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3300 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) [3300.] Section Thirty-three Hundred. For the breach of an obligation arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate the party aggrieved for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, or which, in the ordinary course of things, would be likely to result therefrom. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the recourse for a breach of contract to only termination of the tour, eliminating the possibility of monetary damages or injunctive relief. This directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3300, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that the measure of damages for breach of contract is the amount that will compensate the aggrieved party for the detriment caused by the breach. By restricting the remedy to termination, the changed text prevents the injured party from seeking full compensation as provided by law, therefore creating a legal contradiction.
